speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Venezia, you were talking about how the uprising was planned and how this Polish couple was working with Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mmhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us more about what happened what you found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we were talking all the time with Joseph. And Joseph told us that, be prepared, because who knows. Today, tomorrow, or next week, we're gonna have the signal from the partisans and to make the revolution. What you have to do, he said, is to get some knives-- because we can find knives over there from the transport that were coming-- and get some heavy gloves, and some boots if-- if it's possible-- which we could have it over there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when the time is coming, and we're going to say, we make the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means that time, just the start.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the signal to start?

speaker: interviewee
text: The signal, [NON-ENGLISH]. Then we're going to do the same way, like I said-- the first thing, kill the two guards inside, go outside-- the guards was going to-- to-- to change over there, outside. Get the-- the gloves, German gloves. And get the shotgun and the-- I always forget the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Machine gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: The machine gun. And from there, we're still-- we're going into the Birkenau, and so on. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- can I ask you, what was the signal supposed to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was it? What were you supposed to see? How were you supposed to know what the revolt was beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, because every night, we were preparing already. We had our clothes in, on us. Because we were an our blocks, inside the barracks. And we had the knives. So-- and we were-- we were waiting the-- the order from Joseph to say, now is the time, especially the time when they were passing by. And was expecting that order. So waiting, and waiting, waiting many times, we were ready to do that. The order was not coming. So to take away the knives, hide them again, and so on, and so on it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one year-- one-- one day we heard about it that in Birkenau, inside, they found a woman, or two women, or men. I don't remember. They found some powder, gunpowder, or whatever. And they hang them. Because they found-- at the other side, they-- I don't know-- some voices around. They said that we got some powder, gunpowder, in the crematoriums to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to do the crematoriums, to-- Blow up.

speaker: interviewee
text: To blow up the crematoriums. That's what-- that's what we knew it. But the order was not coming. In the meantime, they hang up those guys over there. At the other side, not too far away from that, when they hang up over there those two guys, or three, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three, four generals, officers-- they came to-- to the crematorium three. There we were, with Dario. So and they called them everybody for an appell. And everybody come in. The most was-- of course, I forgot to say that before that, they took already 100 guys from the crematoriums, Polands-- they were working too long in the Poland. They said they need them somewhere else. And after we learned that that they took them to Auschwitz. In Auschwitz, they killed them with a-- with an injection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now these 100 guys that they took, were these all-- also Sonderkommandos?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Sonderkommando, yeah. Nobody can come into the Sonderkommando, nobody. We didn't have no communication with nobody. When those officers came over there, there all most Greeks over there-- about 200, 300 Greeks. And the Germans start to call numbers. So, so, so, so, so-- 100 numbers, 150 numbers. They put them aside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Greeks knew that they were going to kill them. So one guy of them said-- said, they're gonna killed us. What about the-- we make the [NON-ENGLISH] now? [NON-ENGLISH]! This [NON-ENGLISH] they know about when the Italian was fighting with the Greeks. They were calling, [NON-ENGLISH]. And everybody running to each other. So one of the Greek guys-- he had a shovel [INAUDIBLE], and hit the German right on the head. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The German, with the-- with his hand holding his head, run out and start screaming. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the meantime, those Greeks-- they were over there, all of them-- they put them down, right on the-- they had a big hole in it, over there, somewhere. And they start bam, bam, bam, bam. They killed all of them. The Germans were--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. They killed all of them. It was right after-- after they hear the-- in the crematorium one, in four, and two, we heard the shotguns. We heard the bullets. What's going on? Because in two, three minutes, that crematorium was [INAUDIBLE] already from the SS with the dogs, with the shotguns, with the-- whatever you called them-- in two, three minutes. No-- no more than that. And they killed them right away, over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I know that? Because I was in crematory two. Another cousin of mine-- it's a Venezia, Isaac Venezia-- he ran away from there. He went into the crematorium. And somebody else, again, ran to-- into the crematorium. And he burned some beds way over there. He put fire on it. And the crematorium started to burn. Which crematorium was that, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crematorium three. It was not blow up. They put the fire on it. That Isaac Venezia-- he walks into the crematorium. When he saw the flames, he didn't know what to do. He went on the second floor, when he was hiding, and the flames was coming up. He went to the kind of a bathroom we had over there. And the flames was coming. And he didn't know he was going to get burned. And he started hitting the floor with his feet, so hard, hitting, hitting, hitting, hitting the floor-- broke up the floor on top-- the ceiling, or the floor, whatever-- and he came down. When he came down, he run right on the electrical wires they had over there. And he was telling me all the way that time-- all the time he was telling me how somebody can get away from-- from those wires. It was those pointed wires they got, like this. He was telling me, just lay down like this and just make a turn. That way, holding a little bit the wires, you can get out. This is the way he did. Because they brought him to us after. And I learned it from him, the way they killed the other guys and they-- they push them in the hole. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What he did-- he got away from this wire, from the crematorium around the wires. What did-- where did he go? Did he go to Kanada kommando. He was near to it. So [INAUDIBLE] when they saw him. They got him, and they hide him. They hide him one, two days. And then the guard, the German guard, went inside. And they told him, we got a guy, this and [INAUDIBLE]. This German, he didn't killed them. He brought him to us in crematorium two. And he was working with us. Now with the crematorium building over there, the [INAUDIBLE] crematorium one, crematorium two, and crematorium four. Crematorium four, they killed them right away. From the crematorium one was in the south side, I guess, or north side-- south side or north side. So they cut the wires, because it was preparing for that, for that [INAUDIBLE]. They cut the wires, the electrical wires. And they start to run out, everybody on the-- there was too many trees. What they call them? The forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the forest. Because after the forest, over there, in about maybe 10 kilometers, or five kilometers, or 15 kilometers, there was another guard of Germans. They were running out from there, the crematorium one. The crematorium two, we couldn't do nothing, because they [? circled ?] us right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were surrounded-- you mean you were surrounded by soldier--

speaker: interviewee
text: Surrounded from the Germans, yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We were preparing for those, all those things, one of the Russian-- Malenkov, Malenkov-- one of those Russians of the 18, Malenkov name-- a very strong guy, tall. What he did-- he went, he punch the two wheels of the bicycle the German, not to let him get out and call for help. In the meantime, we were [INAUDIBLE]. When the Germans came-- the Germans, I told you, was a red one, who was asking-- who was working as a Sonderkommando in Mauthausen. That's the same German. He got his cane. And he ask, who punch his bicycle? And he said, I did. And with the cane, he killed him, with the cane-- hit him on his head. And that was the end of this Malenkov. In the meantime, crematorium one, over there was this kapo, the German, [INAUDIBLE] the name, which was one of the worst kapos we ever had over there, beating to everybody for nothing, just passing by by him, with the cane, bam, on your head. A couple guys of us-- two, three guys of us-- they got him. They knife him-- right away, on the oven. That was the end of this [INAUDIBLE]. Now, in the meantime, from crematorium one they start to run out. Everybody got killed. And everybody-- they took them back with some big holes on their heads, like this. We are only 100 guys in crematorium two remaining. They didn't touch us. They got us to crematorium one, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to burn the dead people that they want-- they scared. They needed us at that time. That's why they couldn't kill us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you something. In your group of people, in your group of Sonderkommandos-- did you have any guns, or any ammunition, or gun powder?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No gunpowder, no ammunition, no nothing-- just knives. We-- we-- we filed all day. The knife. We had a way to file to file the knives. We made some sharp knives. That's all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this Joseph, who was planning the revolt, Joseph Baruch, how was he in contact with the partisans? Do you know how?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. That's [INAUDIBLE] they were saying. Actually, they were not in-- in contact with this Joseph. They were in contact with our Poland kapo. That's what they were saying to us. I never saw anything. The only thing I-- is I heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while the revolt was going on around you, and you were surrounded by the SS, were you able to do anything, get out at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We couldn't do nothing. They were over everybody, all the Germans over there, with the-- with their trucks and machine guns. What could you do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit. And let me ask you more about the Sonderkommandos. Were you aware of a regular schedule for killing the Sonderkommandos after they worked for a certain period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The only time we saw killing the Sonderkommando is when the Hungarian transport stops and they didn't need it, so many people work in crematorias. In the meantime, I forgot to tell you, before the revolution, when we saw the gypsies [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ghetto over there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one day they took down-- they took all the men. They were over there, I don't know, 50, 60, 100 men. They took them away. And we learned that they took them to Auschwitz. They killed them over there with the same way, with the [? injection. ?] At the same night, after they took the men from there, they brought to us all the women and the children that were with us. And they killed them right in the crematoriums. And we burned them over there. That was the end of the gypsies, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they killed them in crematorium, how did they kill them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, at that time, they kill them with the-- with the gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Earlier, you mentioned something about sending your soup to the children's camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with those children? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those children-- of course, we didn't see them. And never we saw them, because we were never in contact with them. But the 100 people remaining from the crematorias-- they needed us to-- to burn some other dead people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At the end, they put us to make some holes from the crematorias, like I show you in the picture of my brother over there, that you don't see nothing, because they blew up the crematorium over there. That's why you cannot see nothing. And they brought some other people from Auschwitz to help us in a rush, to do some [INAUDIBLE] on the cements, on the walls, and so on. We knew it, that they want to put some dyna-- dynama-- dynamite to blow up the crematoriums, which it took three, four days, or five days, to do all those holes. As a matter of fact, they told us, if you make so many holes-- because we were two guys making a hole, one holding the-- that kind of tool to make the hole, the other one with a heavy hammer hitting on top. If you make two, three holes, or four holes, or five holes a day, then you can rest-- which everybody want to do the holes faster, just do not work anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after they make the holes and everything else, they took us-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, they took us-- yeah, they-- they took us to inside the Birkenau to the regular barracks. We were [INAUDIBLE] over the crematoriums working. And we-- we put us-- they put us in one barrack, inside. And they locked up the doors. Now, over there we were a few Greeks and few Polands-- all the ones working in the crematoriums. Now, we were thinking that they locked us over here because they're gonna killed us now. They don't need us anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, we saw some flames at the entrance of the-- of Birkenau, some flames. That was all the-- the papers they had over there, the officers and so on-- everything on flame. At the other side, we saw for a-- from a window that too many kids, children with with no numbers on it, walking and getting out. And some other sick people-- they couldn't kill them, because they didn't want to kill them. They couldn't kill them, because the Russian was so near, they didn't want to leave bodies on the-- on the side, to let the Russians see all those-- all those bodies, something unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we are in the barrack. And we saw everybody. It was almost night. Everybody going out. Before they-- they're hanging us over there, locked up over here. The Greek people, again, just broke the doors, as we say, and get out. The Poland was afraid-- they're going to kill us. But anyway, they're going to killed us. Because they didn't want to leave anybody working in the Sonderkommando to be alive, just to test-- testify, like I'm testifying now. That was the idea of the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end, the Greek people didn't-- they couldn't stand it anymore. We blow up the-- we-- we-- we broke the-- the doors. And we went out, and we say together, don't say nobody that we are Sonderkommando, and get mixed with the kids, with the sick people, with the old people, so and so, so, so, so. Get [INAUDIBLE] hidden there, hidden there, hidden there. And this is what we did, Carol. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got out. And we got mixed with some other-- not together the Sonderkommando. Here and there, here and there, and we got out. And we went-- they took us to Auschwitz. In the meantime, when we got out from the barrack, we were hungry. Because there was not transports anymore to get-- [LAUGHS] to get food or to get to-- to get the [INAUDIBLE]. The first thing we did [INAUDIBLE] to go in the kitchen was in Birkenau. In the kitchen over there, when I went inside there, I didn't find nothing but one kilo of a kind of a German bread. And it was all molded, all molded. It didn't take not even to five minutes. I ate it, all of it. No matter how much you would-- you would eat another time, you wouldn't have food. So this way, they took us to Auschwitz. From Auschwitz, the same night, we had a blanket-- each one of us. We start walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- before we-- we start talking about that, let me take you back, just a little bit. First, do you remember when this was, what time of year, and what year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't ask me dates. I don't remember nothing, Carol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it wintertime? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Winter. It was winter. Because winter-- it was winter. And we was walking. And we had a blanket on our shoulder. And we could not walk anymore with the-- because that blanket was getting heavier, and heavier, and heavier. Even with that cold, many people were throwing away the blanket. Because we were exhausted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the SS took you in Birkenau, and they put you into this barracks and locked the doors, who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the 100 people working in the Sonderkommando. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, Dario, his brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you mixed in with the rest of the camp members to go to Auschwitz, were you all still together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we dispersed. We dispersed here and there. Because if not, if the Germans knew that the Sonderkommando went out from that barracks, they were going to-- to search for us and killed us. That's why we-- we dispersed. That's the only way it was-- from 1,200 we were working in the crematorium, we live-- we lived only 100.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got to Auschwitz, who was-- who were these people leaving with? Who were they leaving the camp with to go on this march? Were there SS guarding them? How did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the-- all the time there were the SS guarding. And we walk, walk, and walk, and walk, and walk. On the end, we found out that they found some trains to take us to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: On this march--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how many people would you say were walking together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? Thousands of people. Thousands I can tell. And everybody who was at the end, if you could not walk anymore, bam, bam. You could see, right on the snow, so many-- so many people killed from the guard at the end. The people cannot walk-- who could walk anymore, they were killed. Many people was praying to get killed because they could not stand anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're walking, and walking, and walking, and walking, I'm telling you, all exhausted at the end. We stop-- we pass by a-- by a little river, I guess-- something like that-- and so hungry. We-- we saw some snails around, in the water. We-- we got the snails and we swallowed the snails the way they was. And we stop at the end on a barn. It was a barn, because there was some rooms, like this for the animals they were put in, and a big lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who got into those rooms, it was lucky that night. I was the last one to enter by, because they could not enter no one-- no one else. Because they were all together, because it was so cold. So and many people stays on the lot at that time. And they slept over there, outside. In the morning, when we went out, nobody was alive. Everybody was frozen.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were marching, did you come in contact-- were you able to see any of the people living in the countryside, the villagers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. At the end, from there, they took us from some trains, like I tell you-- I told you-- train-- open trains. I don't know how many days we were. It was going, stop, going, and stop. We were coming-- when the train stops, we used to jump off the wagon over there, to go and get some snow for making a little water from the snow. And the SS shooting on us, and killing, but people wanted to drink something. Except they-- hungry they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these open trains, and snow, with the blankets on our heads like this, until we went to Czechoslovakia. In Czechoslovakia were those guys, which I will never forget, throwing us bottles of hot water, or tea, or a piece of bread-- throwing us, and the Germans shooting on them. But still, they were throwing us. Maybe who knows how many lives they saved for, with this water and bread? Who were those people? Who were those people who were throwing you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakian. Maybe they were Communist. Who knows? What's the different? They were people, people thinking about the [INAUDIBLE]. That don't mean because they-- they were Communist, they're not human beings. I had so many friends Communist, which are human beings, are very good people. Just they got their own ideas, their own [INAUDIBLE], their own-- like anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food were they throwing at the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bread, mostly. The most bread. Then, from then-- from there-- I'm not finished yet. [LAUGHS] You want to come tomorrow, again? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. So from then, they took us to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long would you say the trip took-- walking and the trains together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? Five days. Who knows? We were not able to walk, anyway. At one [INAUDIBLE] we were walking at that time it was-- if you're walking at that time it was a big deal. Because we were so-- like I told you, when I got liberated was-- my weight was 45 or 50 pounds. I couldn't stand up. Anyway, too many people got killed at that time, because of walking and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrive in Mauthausen-- cold, snowing. Where we went over there, we took off our clothes. They put them in a-- disinfectant rooms, and so on. And for two minutes, we give us a hot shower-- hot shower. I mean, and coming a little bit from a-- from a tube it was coming a little bit water-- hot shower. And then, naked, we made us walking in a hole, to get out. The hole-- all the windows in the one side all open, with that cold to kill us. So we went outside, all naked. And everybody shaking from the cold. And what became over there-- ice on the-- on the ground. What happened over there was that everybody was cold. We were goin' in a circle. So one, with each other, to get warmer, to-- what happened over there are they now is the one they were in the center, the outside, the were [INAUDIBLE] they were going around in the center. The one who were in the center were coming out. I don't know how many hours-- two, three hours, four hours we staying over there, naked, with that snow and that cold. Afterward, and they got us out of there, I don't know how many dead bodies it was right on the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us from there. They put us in a barrack. The barrack was not big enough to hold all of us-- still naked. And they made us sit down on the floor in lines, like this, one knee to the other, like this-- lines, lines, lines, lines. At that night, this-- came that guy, the SS guy, the red one, that we used to call him [? red-- ?] a big tall guy who killed the-- the-- the Russian, Malenkov. And he was looking for the Sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I saw him, Carol, I got so scared. Maybe he is going to recognize me. I had near to me two guys, which they are in Israel now. I don't know if they're still alive or not-- two brothers. They were sitting behind me. When I told them that I was working in the crematoriums, and that's the guy who killed many people, and so on, so on, so now-- now, who knows? He wants to look-- he's looking for the Sonderkommando people-- what they did is they made a little room. I went flat on the floor. And they sit on top of me. We're gonna stop right there and we're gonna change the tape.
